steps.content: Functions That Come with Python

Python ships with dozens of built-in functions — no import needed. Beyond the basics like print(), len(), and type(), there's a collection of powerful functions that make common tasks cleaner and more readable.
All the functions in this lesson are available without any import. They are always ready to use — one of the best parts of learning Python!
enumerate — Number Your Items
When you loop through a list and need to know each item's position, enumerate() is your friend. It returns pairs of (index, item), saving you from manually tracking a counter variable.
By default, enumerate() starts counting at 0. You can pass a second argument to change the starting number: enumerate(fruits, start=1).
zip — Pair Up Lists
The zip() function pairs up items from multiple iterables. If you have two lists and want to process them together, zip() creates tuples of matching items.

names = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie']
scores = [85, 92, 78]

for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f'{name}: {score}')
If the lists are different lengths, zip() stops at the shortest one. Use itertools.zip_longest() if you need to keep going.
sorted and reversed
The sorted() function returns a new sorted list from any iterable, without modifying the original. The reversed() function returns an iterator that goes through items in reverse order.

numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2]

print('Sorted:', sorted(numbers))
print('Reversed:', list(reversed(numbers)))
print('Original:', numbers)
any, all, and isinstance

any() returns True if at least one item in an iterable is truthy. all() returns True only if every item is truthy. isinstance() checks whether a value is of a specific type — cleaner than comparing type().

checks = [True, False, True]
print('Any true?', any(checks))
print('All true?', all(checks))

print(isinstance(42, int))
print(isinstance('hello', list))
min, max, and sum
These three functions do exactly what you'd expect. min() returns the smallest item, max() returns the largest, and sum() adds up all the numbers in an iterable. Simple, but incredibly useful.

grades = [88, 92, 76, 95, 83]

print('Highest:', max(grades))
print('Lowest:', min(grades))
print('Total:', sum(grades))
print('Average:', sum(grades) / len(grades))
Most of these functions accept an optional key argument that lets you customize the comparison. For example, max(words, key=len) returns the longest word instead of the alphabetically last one.
